items.text: Are logs emitted in a structured format (JSON) rather than free-text strings?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Log entries are structured key-value JSON objects rather than concatenated strings, making them parseable by log aggregation tools.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Free-text logs require fragile regex parsing in tools like Elasticsearch or CloudWatch. Structured logs enable filtering, aggregation, and alerting on specific fields.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check logger configuration for JSON formatters
- Verify log statements use structured fields instead of string interpolation
- Ensure the log output includes standard fields (timestamp, level, message, service)
baseContent.exampleComment: This log uses string interpolation which makes it impossible to filter by userId in our log aggregation tool. Use structured fields instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: logger.info(`User ${userId} performed ${action} on ${resource}`);

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Structured logs are searchable, filterable, and aggregatable — free-text logs are not.

items.text: Are log levels used correctly (error for failures, warn for degraded, info for events, debug for diagnostics)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each log statement uses the appropriate severity level: error for failures needing attention, warn for degraded but functional states, info for significant events, debug for development diagnostics.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Incorrect log levels cause alert fatigue (too many errors) or missed incidents (errors logged as warnings). Production typically filters to info and above.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that caught-and-handled exceptions use warn, not error
- Verify error level is reserved for actionable failures
- Ensure debug-level logs don't contain PII that could leak in debug mode
baseContent.exampleComment: This logs every cache miss as an error. Cache misses are expected behavior — use info or debug level instead. Reserve error for actual failures.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
logger.error('Cache miss for key: ' + key);
logger.info('Database connection failed', { error });

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
logger.debug('cache miss', { key });
logger.error('database connection failed', { error: error.message, host });

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Reserve error for actionable failures — misused levels cause alert fatigue or missed incidents.

items.text: Are logs free of sensitive data (passwords, tokens, PII)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Log statements do not include passwords, API keys, session tokens, credit card numbers, or other personally identifiable information.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Logs are often stored in systems with broader access than production databases. Sensitive data in logs creates compliance violations (GDPR, PCI-DSS) and security risks.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Search log statements for fields like password, token, secret, ssn, creditCard
- Check that request/response logging redacts sensitive headers (Authorization, Cookie)
- Verify error logs don't dump full request bodies that may contain PII
baseContent.exampleComment: This logs the full request body which may contain the user's password. Redact sensitive fields before logging, or log only specific safe fields.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
logger.info('login attempt', { body: req.body });
// logs: { email: '...', password: 'hunter2' }

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code: logger.info('login attempt', { email: req.body.email });

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never log secrets or PII — logs are accessible to many people and systems.

items.text: Do logs include correlation/request IDs for tracing requests across services?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every log entry includes a correlation ID (request ID, trace ID) that ties together all log entries for a single user request across multiple services.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without correlation IDs, debugging a single request in a distributed system requires guessing which log lines belong together based on timestamps and content.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that middleware injects a requestId or traceId into the logging context
- Verify the correlation ID is propagated to downstream service calls
- Search for log statements that might bypass the shared context
baseContent.exampleComment: These log entries don't include a request ID. When debugging production issues, we won't be able to trace a single request across services.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: logger.info('processing order', { orderId });

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Correlation IDs are essential for tracing a single request through distributed systems.

items.text: Do log entries include sufficient context to understand what happened without reading code?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Log messages include enough contextual fields (who, what, where, result) that an on-call engineer can understand the event without referring to source code.
baseContent.whyItMatters: During an incident at 3 AM, engineers need logs that tell the full story. A log that says 'failed' with no context forces engineers to dig through code under pressure.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Read each log statement and ask: could an on-call engineer understand this without context?
- Check that error logs include the operation that failed and relevant identifiers
- Verify success and failure paths both have adequate logging
baseContent.exampleComment: This error log says 'operation failed' but doesn't include which operation, which resource, or the error details. Add context fields so on-call can diagnose without reading code.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: logger.error('operation failed');

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Logs should tell the full story — an on-call engineer shouldn't need source code to understand what happened.

items.text: Are the RED metrics (Rate, Errors, Duration) captured for every service endpoint?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every service endpoint tracks request rate (throughput), error rate (failures), and duration (latency) as core metrics for monitoring service health.
baseContent.whyItMatters: The RED method provides the minimum metrics needed to detect service degradation. Without these, issues can go unnoticed until users report them.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that HTTP middleware or interceptors collect request count, error count, and latency
- Verify metrics are broken down by endpoint, method, and status code
- Ensure histogram buckets for latency are appropriate for the service's SLA
baseContent.exampleComment: This new endpoint doesn't have latency metrics. Our standard middleware should handle this — verify it's applied to this route.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Rate, Errors, and Duration are the minimum metrics for monitoring any service endpoint.

items.text: Do metric names follow a consistent naming convention with units?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Metric names use a consistent pattern (e.g., namespace_subsystem_name_unit) and include the unit of measurement in the name (seconds, bytes, total).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Inconsistent naming makes metrics hard to discover and query. Without units, engineers must guess whether duration is in seconds or milliseconds.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check metric names follow the project's naming convention
- Verify units are included in the name (e.g., _seconds, _bytes, _total)
- Ensure counters end with _total and use base units (seconds, not milliseconds)
baseContent.exampleComment: This metric is named 'request_latency' without units. Rename to 'http_request_duration_seconds' to follow Prometheus conventions.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
const latency = new Histogram({ name: 'request_latency' });
const errors = new Counter({ name: 'num_errors' });

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
const latency = new Histogram({ name: 'http_request_duration_seconds' });
const errors = new Counter({ name: 'http_requests_errors_total' });

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Include units in metric names and follow a consistent naming convention.

items.text: Are metric labels bounded to prevent cardinality explosion?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Metric labels use bounded values (HTTP methods, status code classes, known routes) rather than unbounded values (user IDs, request IDs, email addresses).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Each unique label combination creates a new time series. Unbounded labels (like user IDs) can create millions of series, crashing your metrics backend and causing massive storage costs.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check all metric label values for unbounded cardinality
- Verify URL paths are normalized (e.g., /users/:id, not /users/12345)
- Ensure error messages are categorized, not used as raw label values
baseContent.exampleComment: This metric uses the full URL path as a label, including user IDs. This will create a unique time series per user, causing cardinality explosion. Normalize to /users/:id.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
httpDuration.observe({ route: req.url }, elapsed);
// Creates: /users/123, /users/456, /users/789, ...

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
httpDuration.observe({ route: req.routePattern }, elapsed);
// Creates: /users/:id (single series)

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Unbounded label values will crash your metrics system — always use bounded, normalized values.

items.text: Are histograms used for latency rather than averages or gauges?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Latency and duration metrics use histograms (or summaries) to capture the full distribution, rather than tracking only averages or last-seen values.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Averages hide tail latency. A service with 100ms average latency might have a p99 of 5 seconds, affecting 1% of users. Histograms let you compute percentiles.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that latency metrics use Histogram type, not Gauge or averaged Counter
- Verify histogram buckets cover the expected latency range
- Ensure percentile queries (p50, p95, p99) work correctly
baseContent.exampleComment: This tracks average request duration as a gauge. Use a histogram instead so we can compute p95 and p99 latency for SLO tracking.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
const avgLatency = new Gauge({ name: 'avg_request_duration' });
avgLatency.set(totalDuration / requestCount);

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Histograms capture the full distribution — averages and gauges hide tail latency problems.

items.text: Are business-relevant metrics tracked alongside technical metrics?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The service tracks metrics that reflect business outcomes (orders processed, payments completed, users signed up) in addition to infrastructure metrics.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Technical metrics tell you the system is healthy; business metrics tell you the system is working correctly. A service can be technically healthy while producing wrong results.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Identify the core business actions of the service
- Check that each has at least a counter (total attempts, successes, failures)
- Verify business metrics are distinct from HTTP-level metrics
baseContent.exampleComment: We track HTTP request metrics but not order completion rate. If orders fail silently (returning 200 with an error body), our HTTP metrics won't catch it.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Track business outcomes, not just infrastructure health — a healthy server can still produce wrong results.

items.text: Do metrics align with defined SLIs/SLOs for the service?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The metrics collected can be used to compute the service's Service Level Indicators (SLIs) and track progress toward Service Level Objectives (SLOs).
baseContent.whyItMatters: If your SLO is 99.9% of requests under 500ms, but you don't have a histogram with a 500ms bucket, you can't measure your SLO compliance.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Review the service's defined SLOs
- Verify histogram buckets include the SLO thresholds
- Check that error classification matches the SLO definition (e.g., 5xx only vs all errors)
baseContent.exampleComment: Our SLO targets p99 under 200ms, but the histogram buckets jump from 100ms to 500ms. Add a 200ms bucket so we can accurately track our SLO.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Metrics must be designed to answer SLO questions — align histogram buckets and error definitions with your SLOs.

items.text: Is trace context propagated across all service boundaries (HTTP, gRPC, message queues)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: W3C Trace Context headers (traceparent, tracestate) or equivalent are propagated in all outgoing requests, including HTTP calls, gRPC calls, and message queue publications.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Broken propagation creates disconnected traces. If service A calls service B but doesn't forward the trace context, the traces appear as separate unrelated operations.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that HTTP clients inject trace context headers
- Verify message queue producers include trace context in message metadata
- Test end-to-end: does a single trace span the full request lifecycle?
baseContent.exampleComment: This HTTP client call to the payment service doesn't propagate trace context. The payment service trace will be disconnected from the parent trace.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Trace context must be propagated across every service boundary or the distributed trace breaks.

items.text: Are spans named descriptively with consistent conventions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Span names follow a consistent pattern (e.g., 'HTTP GET /users', 'db.query users', 'queue.publish orders') that makes them easy to identify and group in tracing UIs.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Poorly named spans make traces unreadable. Spans named 'operation' or 'execute' provide no context when debugging latency in a trace waterfall.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check span names for descriptive, consistent naming
- Verify they include the operation type and target
- Ensure span names don't include unbounded values (user IDs, query parameters)
baseContent.exampleComment: This span is named 'execute'. Rename it to something descriptive like 'db.query.find_user_by_email' so it's meaningful in trace views.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
tracer.startSpan('execute');
tracer.startSpan(`query-${userId}`);

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
tracer.startSpan('db.query find_user_by_email');
tracer.startSpan('http.client POST /payments/charge');

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Span names should be descriptive and bounded — they're the labels in your trace waterfall.

items.text: Are errors and exceptions recorded on spans with appropriate status codes?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When an operation fails, the span is marked with an error status and the exception details are recorded as span events or attributes.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without error recording on spans, failed operations appear successful in the tracing UI. Engineers can't filter for error traces or see where failures occurred.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that catch blocks record the exception on the active span
- Verify span status is set to ERROR when operations fail
- Ensure error details (message, type) are captured without sensitive data
baseContent.exampleComment: This catch block logs the error but doesn't record it on the span. The trace will show this operation as successful. Use span.recordException() and span.setStatus().

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always record exceptions on spans — otherwise failed operations appear successful in traces.

items.text: Do spans include relevant attributes for debugging (IDs, parameters, results)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Spans include contextual attributes like entity IDs, operation parameters, and result metadata that help engineers understand the operation's context during debugging.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A span that says 'db.query' with no attributes forces engineers to correlate with logs. Rich attributes make traces self-contained for debugging.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that spans include relevant business identifiers (orderId, userId)
- Verify database spans include the table/collection name
- Ensure no sensitive data is included in span attributes
baseContent.exampleComment: This database span doesn't include the table name or query type. Add attributes like db.table and db.operation so we can identify slow queries in traces.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Rich span attributes make traces self-contained for debugging — no need to cross-reference logs.

items.text: Is a sampling strategy configured to balance cost and visibility?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The tracing configuration uses a sampling strategy (head-based, tail-based, or adaptive) to control the volume of traces collected, balancing observability with storage costs.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Collecting 100% of traces at scale is prohibitively expensive. But sampling too aggressively means missing rare error traces. A good strategy captures errors and a representative sample of normal traffic.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check the tracing SDK configuration for sampling settings
- Verify error traces are always sampled (not dropped)
- Ensure the sampling rate is appropriate for the service's traffic volume
baseContent.exampleComment: This service has no sampling configuration, meaning it will collect 100% of traces. At our traffic volume, this will overwhelm the tracing backend. Configure a sampling strategy.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Configure sampling to capture all errors and a representative sample — 100% collection doesn't scale.

items.text: Are all spans properly ended, including in error paths?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every span that is started is also ended (span.end()), including when exceptions occur. Unfinished spans leak memory and create incomplete traces.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Spans that are never ended consume memory indefinitely, create incomplete traces in the UI, and can distort latency metrics. This is especially common in error paths.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that span.end() is called in finally blocks
- Look for early returns that bypass span.end()
- Verify async operations end their spans after the async work completes, not before
baseContent.exampleComment: This span is started but not ended in the catch block. If an error occurs, the span leaks. Move span.end() to a finally block.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always end spans in a finally block to prevent leaks in error paths.

items.text: Does every alert have a clear action the on-call engineer should take?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each alert includes a description of what's wrong, why it matters, and what the on-call engineer should do. Alerts that require no action should not page anyone.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Non-actionable alerts cause alert fatigue. Engineers learn to ignore alerts, and real incidents get missed. Every page should require and describe a specific human action.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check alert definitions for a runbook link or action description
- Verify the alert describes the symptom, not just the metric name
- Ensure informational alerts use low-priority channels (Slack), not paging
baseContent.exampleComment: This alert fires when CPU exceeds 80% but doesn't say what to do. Is this expected during batch jobs? Add a runbook link and clarify whether this requires action.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every paging alert must answer: what's wrong, why it matters, and what should I do right now?

items.text: Are alerts based on symptoms (user impact) rather than causes (resource utilization)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Alerts fire based on user-facing symptoms (high error rate, slow response times) rather than internal causes (high CPU, queue depth) that may or may not indicate a problem.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Cause-based alerts generate false positives (CPU spike during deploy is normal) and miss real issues (low CPU but all requests returning errors). Symptom-based alerts catch actual user impact.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check if alerts measure user-facing metrics (error rate, latency SLO)
- Verify cause-based alerts (CPU, memory) are low-priority or informational
- Ensure alerts don't fire for transient spikes that auto-resolve
baseContent.exampleComment: This alert fires on memory usage > 80%. Is this actually causing user impact? Consider alerting on error rate or latency instead, and using memory as a diagnostic metric.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Alert on symptoms (errors, latency) not causes (CPU, memory) — causes are for diagnosis, not paging.

items.text: Are alert thresholds based on data, not guesses, with appropriate burn rate windows?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Alert thresholds are derived from historical baseline data and use appropriate time windows to avoid false positives from brief spikes.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Thresholds set too low cause constant paging; too high means missing real incidents. Short windows fire on brief spikes; long windows delay detection.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that thresholds are justified by historical data or SLO math
- Verify alerts use appropriate evaluation windows (e.g., 5-minute average, not instant)
- Ensure burn-rate alerts are used for SLO-based alerting
baseContent.exampleComment: This alert fires if any single request exceeds 1 second. Individual slow requests are normal. Use a threshold like 'p95 latency > 1s over 5 minutes' instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Base thresholds on historical data and SLO math — guessed thresholds cause alert fatigue.

items.text: Do alerts link to up-to-date runbooks or dashboards?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each alert definition includes a link to a runbook documenting investigation and remediation steps, and a link to the relevant monitoring dashboard.
baseContent.whyItMatters: At 3 AM, an on-call engineer receiving an alert they've never seen needs a runbook to guide their response. Without one, MTTR increases dramatically.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check each alert definition for a runbook_url or dashboard_url annotation
- Verify the linked runbook is current and accurate
- Ensure the dashboard shows the metrics relevant to the alert
baseContent.exampleComment: This new alert has no runbook link. Add a runbook URL so on-call engineers know how to investigate and remediate when this fires.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every alert needs a runbook — on-call engineers should never have to start from scratch.

items.text: Are alerts deduplicated to prevent notification storms?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Alert rules are configured with deduplication, grouping, and inhibition to prevent multiple alerts from firing for the same underlying issue.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A single database outage can trigger alerts from every service that depends on it. Without deduplication, the on-call engineer gets 50 pages instead of 1.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check alerting tool configuration for grouping rules
- Verify inhibition rules suppress downstream alerts when upstream fires
- Test that a single failure doesn't generate multiple independent alerts
baseContent.exampleComment: If the database goes down, this will fire a separate alert for each of our 12 services. Configure alert inhibition so the database alert suppresses the downstream service alerts.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Group and deduplicate alerts to prevent notification storms during cascading failures.

items.text: Does the service dashboard show the four golden signals (latency, traffic, errors, saturation)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The primary service dashboard shows latency distributions, request rate, error rate, and resource saturation (CPU, memory, connections) in a single view.
baseContent.whyItMatters: The four golden signals provide a complete health picture at a glance. Missing any one of them means on-call engineers have to hunt through multiple dashboards during incidents.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Open the service dashboard and check for latency, traffic, errors, and saturation panels
- Verify latency shows percentiles, not just averages
- Ensure error rate is shown as a percentage, not just absolute counts
baseContent.exampleComment: This service dashboard shows latency and errors but not traffic or saturation. Add request rate and resource utilization panels for a complete health overview.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every service dashboard should show the four golden signals in a single view.

items.text: Do dashboard panels have descriptive titles and units?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each panel has a title that describes what's being measured and includes the unit of measurement (e.g., 'Request Latency (p95, seconds)' not just 'Latency').
baseContent.whyItMatters: Ambiguous panel titles waste time during incidents. 'Latency' could mean p50, p99, or average. Clear titles prevent misinterpretation under pressure.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Read each panel title — does it describe the metric, aggregation, and unit?
- Check that time-series legends are meaningful
- Verify panels have descriptions or tooltips for additional context
baseContent.exampleComment: This panel is titled 'Errors' — is this error count, error rate, or error percentage? Rename to 'Error Rate (5xx responses / total, %)' for clarity.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Panel titles should include what's measured, how it's aggregated, and the unit.

items.text: Do dashboard panels use consistent time ranges and refresh intervals?

baseContent.whatItMeans: All panels on a dashboard share the same time range and refresh interval, and the default time range is appropriate for the dashboard's purpose.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Panels with different time ranges create a misleading picture. A latency spike in one panel might not align with the error panel if they show different windows.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that all panels use the dashboard's time range variable
- Verify the default time range makes sense (e.g., 1 hour for incident response, 24 hours for trends)
- Ensure auto-refresh is enabled at an appropriate interval
baseContent.exampleComment: Panel 3 is hardcoded to a 24-hour window while the rest use the dashboard time picker. Change it to use the shared time range variable.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: All panels should use the same time range to avoid misleading correlations.

items.text: Do dashboards support drill-down from overview to detail?

baseContent.whatItMeans: High-level overview dashboards link to detailed dashboards for specific services, endpoints, or infrastructure components. Clicking an anomaly takes you deeper.
baseContent.whyItMatters: During incidents, engineers need to move from 'something is wrong' to 'here's the specific problem' quickly. Drill-down links reduce the time to diagnosis.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check overview panels for clickable links to detailed dashboards
- Verify drill-down preserves the selected time range
- Ensure detailed dashboards exist for each service shown in the overview
baseContent.exampleComment: This service overview shows error rates per service but doesn't link to individual service dashboards. Add drill-down links so engineers can quickly investigate specific services.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Overview dashboards should link to detailed views — reduce clicks to diagnosis.

items.text: Is there a panel showing SLO compliance and remaining error budget?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The dashboard includes a panel showing current SLO compliance percentage and remaining error budget for the reporting period.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without SLO visibility, teams can't balance feature velocity against reliability. Error budget panels make reliability trade-offs visible and data-driven.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check for an SLO compliance panel showing current performance vs target
- Verify error budget burn rate is visible
- Ensure the SLO period (30 days rolling, calendar month) is clear
baseContent.exampleComment: This dashboard has detailed metrics but no SLO tracking. Add a panel showing our 99.9% availability SLO compliance and remaining error budget.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Make SLO compliance and error budget visible on the primary service dashboard.

items.text: Are deployment events annotated on dashboard timelines?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Deployment timestamps are overlaid on dashboard graphs as vertical markers, making it easy to correlate changes in metrics with code deployments.
baseContent.whyItMatters: The most common cause of production issues is a recent deployment. Without deployment markers, engineers waste time checking deploy logs to correlate metric changes.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check dashboard configuration for annotation sources
- Verify deployment events appear as markers on time-series panels
- Ensure markers include the deployment version or commit SHA
baseContent.exampleComment: This dashboard doesn't show deployment markers. Add annotations from our CI/CD system so we can quickly correlate metric changes with deployments.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Deployment markers on dashboards instantly answer 'did someone deploy recently?' during incidents.
